questions.stem: A 12-YEAR-OLD BOY with a well-defined NON-SCALY, DEPIGMENTED patch on the DORSUM OF HIS RIGHT FOREARM ONLY. No lesions elsewhere on body.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HYPOPIGMENTED (not fully depigmented), slightly scaly, ill-defined. Common on cheeks in children. NOT complete depigmentation as seen here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: SEGMENTAL VITILIGO: complete chalk-white depigmentation, well-defined, unilateral, dermatomal. Common in young patients. Stabilises earlier than non-segmental. KEY: well-defined + complete depigmentation + single segment = segmental vitiligo.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Malassezia furfur infection → HYPOPIGMENTED/hyperpigmented SCALY patches. KOH: spaghetti-and-meatball hyphae. Usually on trunk, not forearm.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Single HYPOPIGMENTED patches with SENSORY LOSS and thickened peripheral nerves. Not completely depigmented.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: HYPERPIGMENTED (brown). Face only. Pregnancy/OCP-associated. Cannot be depigmented patch on forearm of a 12yo boy.

questions.stem: Same case. If lesion were WIDESPREAD involving a wide area of the body — best treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: First-line for LOCALISED (<10% BSA). Not appropriate for widespread (systemic absorption risk, atrophy, striae).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: May halt progression temporarily but does NOT repigment skin. Unacceptable side-effect profile long-term.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Excellent for localised vitiligo (especially face/neck, sensitive areas). Not suitable for widespread disease.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: NARROW-BAND UVB = GOLD STANDARD for widespread vitiligo. Stimulates follicular melanocytes → perifollicular repigmentation. 2-3 sessions/week. Safer than PUVA (no psoralen needed). Most effective on face/trunk.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Targeted for localised, resistant patches. Not for widespread disease.

questions.stem: What is the SOURCE OF REPIGMENTATION in vitiligo?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: FOLLICULAR MELANOCYTES (hair follicle bulge) = primary source. NBUVB stimulates them → proliferate and migrate outward → perifollicular dots. LEUKOTRICHIA (white hair) = follicular melanocytes destroyed = POOR repigmentation prognosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Epidermal melanocytes are DESTROYED in vitiligo. Cannot serve as repigmentation source.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cosmetic camouflage only. Not biological repigmentation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Surgical treatment option but NOT the spontaneous physiological source.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PIH = HYPERPIGMENTATION after inflammation. Different condition entirely.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cholinergic Urticaria | Q118: Histamine (primary mast cell mediator)

questions.topic: Q1-5 | Bipolar Disorder — Manic Episode with Psychosis
questions.stem: 16-YEAR-OLD MALE: Caught by police before jumping from bridge. Believes he CAN FLY. Assessment: DELUSIONS + HALLUCINATIONS + ELATED MOOD + 2 SUICIDAL ATTEMPTS. Q1 — Diagnosis

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Schizophrenia = flat/inappropriate mood, NOT elated. Mood elevation is not a feature. ≥6 months required.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lifelong pervasive patterns. NOT episodic psychosis with elated mood and grandiose delusions.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: BIPOLAR I = ≥1 manic episode. This patient: elated mood + grandiose delusion (can fly) + hallucinations + impulsive suicidal attempts = MANIC EPISODE WITH PSYCHOTIC FEATURES. Mood-congruent psychosis = bipolar.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: MDD = LOW/sad mood. This patient has ELEVATED mood.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anxiety disorders Q2 — Treatment of Choice
choices.explanation: No psychosis, no elated mood in anxiety disorders.

questions.stem: Mother had GDM (diet-controlled) in previous pregnancy. How to screen for GDM in FUTURE pregnancies?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: GDM is often ASYMPTOMATIC. Prior GDM = 50-70% recurrence. Universal early screening required.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: HIGH-RISK women (prior GDM) need screening EARLIER — at first visit, not only at 24-28 weeks.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ACOG/NICE: prior GDM → OGTT at FIRST prenatal visit (early screening) + if normal → rescreen 24-28 weeks. 50-70% recurrence justifies early diagnosis and intervention.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HbA1C detects PRE-EXISTING T2DM, not GDM. Affected by physiological anaemia of pregnancy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: No screening unless risk factors develop
choices.explanation: Previous GDM IS the major risk factor. Waiting = missed diagnosis.

questions.stem: 25-YEAR-OLD UNMARRIED MALE: 3 weeks of GENERALISED PAPULOSQUAMOUS RASH. NON-PRURITIC. Involves PALMS AND SOLES. GENERALISED LYMPHADENOPATHY.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. PRURITIC. Chronic progressive patches. Does not characteristically involve palms/soles.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Silvery scaly plaques on extensor surfaces. Usually pruritic. No generalised lymphadenopathy. Palmoplantar psoriasis exists but no lymphadenopathy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CLASSIC: NON-PRURITIC papulosquamous rash on PALMS AND SOLES (pathognomonic) + GENERALISED RUBBERY LYMPHADENOPATHY. 6-12 weeks after primary chancre. Treponema pallidum. Sexually active unmarried male = STI context.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Herald patch → Christmas tree trunk rash. Mildly pruritic. DOES NOT involve palms/soles. No lymphadenopathy. Self-limiting.

questions.topic: Cancer Patient on Chemo + Sudden Dyspnoea
questions.stem: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma, 4th cycle chemo: Sudden SHORTNESS OF BREATH. O2 saturation dropped. What is the next step?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CXR cannot diagnose PE (often normal in PE). CTPA required.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ANCER + CHEMO + SUDDEN DYSPNOEA = PE FIRST. Pancreatic Ca = very HIGH TE risk (Trousseau's syndrome = migratory thrombophlebitis). CTPA = gold standard. f PE confirmed → LMWH (preferred over warfarin in cancer — CLOT trial).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: epsis less likely with abrupt sudden onset dyspnoea. PE far more likely in cancer ontext.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: EVER continue chemo with acute dyspnoea and desaturation. Medical emergency equiring investigation.

questions.stem: 15-YEAR-OLD GIRL: 6 months cyclic lower abdominal pain (CRYPTOMENORRHOEA). No menarche (PRIMARY AMENORRHOEA). Examination: BLUISH BULGING MEMBRANE at VAGINAL INTROITUS.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: pain and NO bluish membrane. HAEMATOCOLPOS: menstrual blood retained behind imperforate hymen → BLUISH

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BULGING MEMBRANE (distended with blood). Cyclic pain = cyclical menstruation with NO outlet. Primary amenorrhoea despite normal hormonal function. Treatment: CRUCIATE INCISION → drain haematocolpos. No uterus → no cyclic pain, no bulge. Similar to MRKH.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Sinovaginal bulbs (endodermal outgrowths from urogenital sinus) form lower vagina and

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: hymen. Failure of central perforation → imperforate hymen. Müllerian duct fusion failure → uterine anomalies (not hymen).

questions.stem: 25-YEAR-OLD FEMALE: Painful blisters over body. Multiple FLACCID BULLAE involving body and ORAL CAVITY. Yellow crusted eroded lesions.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TENSE bullae + NO mucosal involvement + elderly. Anti-BP180/230. KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: TENSE + NO MUCOSA = Pemphigoid.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: FLACCID bullae + ORAL MUCOSA (50-70% first site) + Nikolsky sign positive + young-middle age. Anti-Dsg3 (mucosa) + Anti-Dsg1 (skin). Suprabasal intraepidermal split.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Superficial variant. SKIN ONLY — no mucosal involvement. Anti-Dsg1 only (Dsg3 = mucosal protection; Dsg1 = skin).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Genetic, from birth. Not an acquired autoimmune disease. Loss of keratinocyte cohesion (anti-desmoglein destroys desmosomes). TOMBSTONE APPEARANCE of basal cells. DIF: IgG intercellular "CHICKEN WIRE" pattern.

questions.stem: DERMATOLOGY CASE 1: [File page 15 — App-based. Vignette not fully visible in source; case is labelled "E. Contact dermatitis" as the established diagnosis.] Q: Best Investigation? A. KOH | B. WOOD's lamp | C. TPHA | D. VDLR | E. ?? (incomplete in source) Q: Treatment? A. Benzathine pencillin | B. Systemic steroid | C. Topical steroid | D. Systemic antifungal | E. ?? (incomplete in source) ■■ FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING OPTION COMPLETENESS: Options E for both Investigation and Treatment are INCOMPLETE in the source file (marked "??"). Best investigation for contact dermatitis = PATCH TEST, which would logically be Option E (not visible). Treatment E also not visible — likely a specific allergen avoidance option.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: KOH (potassium hydroxide) = used to detect FUNGAL infections (tinea, candida). Dissolves keratin, leaving fungal hyphae visible. NOT for contact dermatitis. Would be correct for: tinea corporis, tinea pedis, candidiasis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Wood's lamp (UV light) = screens for: fungal scalp infections (green fluorescence), vitiligo (bright white), pityriasis versicolor (yellow-gold), erythrasma (coral-red). NOT the investigation for contact dermatitis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: TPHA = confirmatory TREPONEMAL test for SYPHILIS. Completely unrelated to contact dermatitis. Would be correct if diagnosis were secondary syphilis (palms/soles non-pruritic rash).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: VDRL = non-treponemal screening test for SYPHILIS. Used for syphilis screening and monitoring treatment response. Completely unrelated to contact dermatitis.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patch test (incomplete in file) Treatment — Choice Analysis:
choices.explanation: PATCH TEST = GOLD STANDARD for ALLERGIC CONTACT DERMATITIS. Suspected allergen panels applied to back under occlusive tape for 48h → read at 48h and 96h. Identifies specific allergen causing type IV hypersensitivity reaction. Standard panel = TRUE test (nickel, fragrance, rubber, etc.). IRRITANT contact dermatitis = does NOT require patch test (clinical diagnosis).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Benzathine penicillin G = treatment for SYPHILIS (2.4 MU IM single dose for primary/secondary). Completely inappropriate for contact dermatitis. This option is a distractor testing knowledge of STI treatment.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Systemic steroids (prednisolone 0.5-1mg/kg) = used for SEVERE or widespread contact dermatitis that cannot be managed topically. Short course (5-7 days). NOT first-line for localised contact dermatitis.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: TOPICAL CORTICOSTEROIDS = first-line treatment for localised allergic/irritant contact dermatitis. Potency depends on site: face/flexures = mild (1% hydrocortisone); body = moderate-potent (betamethasone valerate); palms/soles = potent (clobetasol propionate). Plus: ALLERGEN AVOIDANCE (most important step).

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Antifungals (fluconazole, terbinafine, itraconazole) = for fungal infections (tinea, candida). Not for contact dermatitis.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Option E text not visible in file. Likely relates to allergen avoidance or emollients/barrier creams.

questions.topic: Neurology Case 3 — Spinal Cord Injury + Bartholin Abscess
questions.stem: NEUROLOGY CASE 3: YOUNG MALE — lower limb weakness + SUDDEN URINE RETENTION after MISSILE TRAUMA. Examination: FLACCID (acute spinal shock phase), AREFLEXIA, SENSORY LEVEL AT T10. OB-GYN CASE: PAINFUL, FLUCTUATING VAGINAL SWELLING at 5-7 O'CLOCK POSITION. Question: diagnosis + cause? Spinal Cord Injury — Lesion Location

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Brainstem lesion: cranial nerve palsies + crossed sensory deficits + respiratory compromise. No sensory level at T10.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pure LMN = peripheral nerve/anterior horn. Focal weakness, no sensory level. The sensory level at T10 indicates CORD, not peripheral nerve.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SPINAL CORD INJURY AT T10 LEVEL: (1) FLACCID + AREFLEXIA = SPINAL SHOCK (acute phase, first 24-72h) (2) After spinal shock resolves → UMN signs return (spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski) (3) SENSORY LEVEL at T10 = umbilicus level (4) URINE RETENTION = neurogenic bladder (detrusor areflexia in spinal shock) Thoracic cord = paraplegia (legs affected, arms intact).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Peripheral nerve injury: focal, no sensory level, no bladder involvement typically. Multiple nerve territories would need to be affected.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome: below L2, saddle anaesthesia, flaccid bladder/bowel, LMN signs in legs. NO definite sensory level at T10 — that implies cord, not cauda equina (cauda equina = below cord end at L1-L2).

questions.stem: OB-GYN CASE: Painful, FLUCTUATING vaginal swelling at 5-7 O'CLOCK POSITION. File confirms this is a Bartholin gland case. Q1: Diagnosis? Q2: Cause?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: BARTHOLIN GLANDS: located at 4-8 o'clock positions (bilateral) at vaginal introitus. Provide lubrication during arousal. BARTHOLIN ABSCESS: blocked duct → infection → fluctuant, TENDER swelling. Unilateral, red, warm, extremely tender. Treatment: MARSUPIALIZATION or Word catheter insertion (not simple I&D; = recurs). Bartholin CYST = non-infected, smooth, non-tender swelling from ductal obstruction. Bartholin ABSCESS = infected, fluctuant, painful, red, warm. This patient has PAINFUL FLUCTUATING = ABSCESS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Ductal obstruction causes CYST (non-infected, not painful, not fluctuant). The abscess develops when the CYST becomes INFECTED. Obstruction is the predisposing factor, not the direct cause of the abscess.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Bartholin abscesses are not congenital. They are acquired.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: BARTHOLIN ABSCESS CAUSE = bacterial infection (POLYMICROBIAL): (1) Skin commensals: E. coli, Staphylococcus, anaerobes (2) STIs: Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis (especially in sexually active women) Treatment: (1) MARSUPIALIZATION = gold standard (creates permanent drainage opening) (2) Word catheter balloon = good alternative for acute abscess (3) Antibiotics (cover STIs + anaerobes) ALWAYS exclude malignancy in women >40y (Bartholin gland carcinoma rare but exists).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Viral infections do not cause classic Bartholin abscess. HSV can cause vulvar ulcers/vesicles but NOT a fluctuant Bartholin abscess.

questions.stem: ELDERLY PATIENT: DECREASED NIGHT VISION but paradoxically notices IMPROVED NEAR VISION while reading his favourite book. Questions: (1) Most common cause? (2) Cause of near vision improvement? (3) Which treatment is useful? (4) Investigation required for lens choice? Q1 — Most Common Cause of his Disease

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SENILE (AGE-RELATED) CATARACT = most common cause of cataract globally (90%+). Caused by: protein aggregation in lens → opacity → yellowing/browning. Subtypes: NUCLEAR (most common in elderly), posterior subcapsular, cortical. Nuclear cataract: hardened nucleus → amber/brown colour → myopic shift.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: In elderly, cataract is age-related (not truly idiopathic). Idiopathic applies when no cause identified in younger patients.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Congenital cataracts: present at birth. Rubella, Toxoplasma, galactosaemia, Down syndrome. Not the cause in an elderly patient.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Secondary cataracts: from DM (snowflake/subcapsular), steroids (PSC), uveitis, trauma. No such history given here.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Metabolic Q2 — Cause of Near Vision Improve
choices.explanation: Metabolic cataracts: DM most common cause. "Snowflake cataract" in DM. Not the most common overall. Shift)

questions.stem: ENT CASE 2: ELDERLY MALE with PERSISTENT COUGH + HOARSENESS + STRIDOR. Q1: Investigation for diagnosis? Q2: If Glottic CA T1N0M0 — best option to preserve voice? Q3: Most recognised risk factor for laryngeal CA? Q1 — Investigation for Diagnosis

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: DIRECT LARYNGOSCOPY (under GA) + BIOPSY = gold standard for laryngeal pathology. Allows: direct visualisation of larynx + representative biopsy for histology. Histology: squamous cell carcinoma (>95% of laryngeal cancers). Also: assess subglottic extension, cord mobility, arytenoid fixation. Supports staging and surgical planning.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CXR may be done to exclude synchronous lung malignancy but is NOT diagnostic for laryngeal cancer. Persistent hoarseness in elderly male → must visualise larynx.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resolves with rest Q2 — Voice-Preserving Treatment f
choices.explanation: NEVER assume persistent hoarseness (>3 weeks in adult) will self-resolve. REFER URGENTLY for laryngoscopy. Hoarseness >3 weeks in adult = red flag = 2-week wait urgent referral in UK (NICE 2015). N0M0

questions.topic: Haematology MCQ: Low Platelets — Least Likely Diagnosis
questions.stem: MCQ: Low platelet level. Which of the following is the LEAST LIKELY diagnosis? A. ITP | B. TTP | C. VWD | D. Haemophilia A | E. Antiphospholipid syndrome

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura = autoimmune destruction of platelets by anti-platelet antibodies (anti-GPIIb/IIIa). Classic cause of isolated thrombocytopenia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura = ADAMTS13 deficiency → microthrombi → platelet consumption → thrombocytopenia. Pentad: MAHA + thrombocytopenia + neurological + renal + fever.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Von Willebrand Disease Type 2B = enhanced binding of abnormal vWF to platelets → platelet clearance → THROMBOCYTOPENIA (can occur). Most VWD types: normal platelet count; Type 2B = exception.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HAEMOPHILIA A = Factor VIII deficiency. PLATELET COUNT = NORMAL in haemophilia. Haemophilia = COAGULATION CASCADE disorder (intrinsic pathway). aPTT prolonged, PT normal, platelets normal. Bleeding: deep haemarthroses, muscle bleeds (not petechiae). KEY: Haemophilia ≠ thrombocytopenia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: APS = antiphospholipid antibodies → THROMBOSIS + obstetric morbidity + THROMBOCYTOPENIA (antibodies also bind platelet phospholipids → destruction).

questions.stem: Difficult to manage at home AND school. Runs around classroom, cannot stay seated, frequent accidents + cuts/bruises. Cannot focus, easily distracted. Cannot wait his turn, intrudes during conversations.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: ADHD DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5): (1) INATTENTION (≥6/9 symptoms): cannot focus, easily distracted, accident-prone (2) HYPERACTIVITY/IMPULSIVITY (≥6/9): runs around, cannot sit still, intrudes, cannot wait turn (3) In MULTIPLE SETTINGS (home AND school) — required for diagnosis (4) BOYS > girls (3:1). Age: <12y for symptom onset. Treatment: Methylphenidate (first-line), behavioural therapy.

questions.stem: Limited vocabulary. No interest in other children. Plays alone. Little eye contact. Does not initiate conversation. Strict BEDTIME RITUAL, angry if disrupted. Tantrums if BEDROOM LAYOUT altered.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ASD FEATURES: (1) SOCIAL COMMUNICATION DEFICIT: little eye contact, no peer interaction, no conversation initiation (2) RESTRICTED/REPETITIVE BEHAVIOURS: strict routine (bedtime ritual), insistence on sameness (bedroom layout) (3) Onset: before age 3 Note: If Hayder has GOOD language + near-normal social interaction → Asperger's syndrome. But limited vocabulary → ASD.

questions.stem: Stomach aches during school days. REFUSES SCHOOL. Cries if mother tries to leave. CANNOT SLEEP without mother. WORRIED MOTHER WILL DIE. Symptoms started when PARENTS DIVORCED 6 months ago.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: SEPARATION ANXIETY DISORDER: (1) EXCESSIVE FEAR of separation from attachment figure (2) Somatic complaints (stomach aches) = somatised anxiety during school (3) School refusal + sleep difficulty without attachment figure (4) Separation anxiety TRIGGERED by parental divorce (stressor) (5) Persistent worry about harm coming to attachment figure (mother dying) Normal up to age 3 — pathological after age 6.

questions.stem: 3 hours each evening CHECKING locks, taps, light switches. REARRANGES FURNITURE. Must perform strict WASHING RITUAL. FEARS parents will come to harm if rituals not performed.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: OCD FEATURES: (1) OBSESSIONS: intrusive fear that parents will be harmed (harm obsession) (2) COMPULSIONS: checking rituals (locks/taps/lights) + washing + rearranging (3) Time-consuming (>1 hour/day = diagnostic threshold) (4) Ego-dystonic (knows it's excessive but can't stop) Treatment: SSRI (fluvoxamine/sertraline) + CBT with ERP.

questions.stem: FREQUENTLY RUDE + ARGUMENTATIVE toward teachers. EASILY ANNOYED. DISRUPTIVE, DISOBEYS classroom rules. NOT aggressive toward children. Has a few friends. NO PROBLEMS AT HOME.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: ODD FEATURES: (1) Angry/irritable mood: easily annoyed (2) Argumentative/defiant: toward AUTHORITY (teachers), disobeys rules (3) NOT conduct disorder (no aggression, destruction, or theft) (4) Has friends (social not impaired) (5) School-specific (not home) — note: ODD usually pervasive (this may suggest he has a specific school issue) KEY DIFFERENTIATOR from Conduct disorder: ODD = no aggression/violation of rights.

questions.stem: Child with BLOODY DISCHARGE from ear + NASAL OBSTRUCTION after period of RESTLESSNESS and FEVER overnight.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: AOM (ASOM): fever + earache + BLOODY/PURULENT DISCHARGE (when TM perforates). Preceded by restlessness and fever = prodromal otitis media. Nasal obstruction: concurrent URTI (congestion) → Eustachian tube dysfunction. Treatment: amoxicillin (if >2 years or severe); watchful waiting (mild, >2yrs).

questions.stem: FARMER with BAD SMELLING (OFFENSIVE) ear discharge + VERTIGO on sneezing or coughing.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CHOLESTEATOMA = keratinising stratified squamous epithelium in middle ear. Features: OFFENSIVE discharge + CONDUCTIVE HEARING LOSS. VERTIGO on sneezing/coughing = FISTULA SIGN (cholesteatoma eroding lateral semicircular canal → perilymph fistula → pneumatic pressure → vertigo). UNSAFE CSOM: offensive + superior/posterior perforation + cholesteatoma. Treatment: MASTOIDECTOMY (surgical excision). Never conservative.

questions.stem: Patient had SURGERY FOR OME. 17 days post-surgery, develops YELLOW DISCHARGE from ear.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: GROMMET INSERTION (tympanostomy tube) = treatment for OME. YELLOW DISCHARGE 17 days post-op = OTORRHOEA THROUGH GROMMET = infected tube. Caused by: water entry, Pseudomonas, Staph aureus. Treatment: topical antibiotic ear drops (ciprofloxacin drops). Grommets fall out spontaneously in 6-12 months.

questions.stem: Patient with SEROPURULENT DISCHARGE from ear. NORMAL TYMPANIC MEMBRANE, NO PERFORATION.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: OTITIS EXTERNA = infection of EXTERNAL EAR CANAL. KEY: NORMAL TM (no middle ear involvement). Discharge from external canal (not through TM perforation). Organisms: Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staph aureus. Treatment: topical antibiotic drops (ciprofloxacin) + steroid. MALIGNANT OE: DM + Pseudomonas + granulation tissue → IV cipro 6-8 weeks.

questions.stem: YOUNG PATIENT had RTA. After 4 DAYS regains consciousness. WATERY DISCHARGE from BOTH EARS.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CSF OTORRHOEA: watery discharge from ears after HEAD TRAUMA = BASAL SKULL FRACTURE (anterior/middle cranial fossa fracture). BILATERAL = more likely CSF than purulent infection. Confirm with: BETA-2 TRANSFERRIN TEST (gold standard, NOT glucose test). Also: Halo sign (ring sign on filter paper), "target sign". Management: most resolve spontaneously; antibiotics controversial; surgical repair if persists >7-10 days.

questions.stem: Increased haematocrit + JAK2 mutation (Polycythaemia Vera)

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: POLYCYTHAEMIA VERA (PV): JAK2 V617F mutation = diagnostic. Increased haematocrit (↑ RBC mass). TREATMENT GOALS: (1) Reduce thrombotic risk (2) Control haematocrit (1) VENESECTION (phlebotomy): first-line. Target Hct <45% (2) ASPIRIN 75mg: reduces thrombotic risk (PV = hypercoagulable) (3) HYDROXYUREA: cytoreductive agent for HIGH-RISK patients (age >60, prior thrombosis). Reduces thrombotic events. HIGH-RISK PV = venesection + aspirin + HYDROXYUREA

choices.label: I
choices.text: ICU+fluid+rasburicase 1. Increased haematocrit + JAK2 mutation 2. 6yo child with frequent painful hand crises 3. 24h after Burkitt lymphoma chemo → drowsy + altered consciousness 4. CLL, 3 days after chemo → dyspnoea + productive cough + fever 5. Patient started ATRA treatment + developed a complication

questions.stem: 6yo child with frequent painful hand crises (Sickle Cell Disease)

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SICKLE CELL DISEASE — PAINFUL CRISES: Mechanism: HbS polymerisation → sickling → vaso-occlusion → ischaemic pain. HYDROXYUREA = #1 disease-modifying treatment for SCD: (1) Increases HbF (fetal haemoglobin) production → inhibits HbS polymerisation (2) Reduces frequency of pain crises by 50% (3) Reduces ACS, stroke, hospitalisations (4) Approved in children ≥9 months (BABY HUG trial) Also: High-dose folic acid, penicillin prophylaxis, vaccinations.

choices.label: I
choices.text: ICU+fluid+rasburicase 1. Increased haematocrit + JAK2 mutation 2. 6yo child with frequent painful hand crises 3. 24h after Burkitt lymphoma chemo → drowsy + altered consciousness 4. CLL, 3 days after chemo → dyspnoea + productive cough + fever 5. Patient started ATRA treatment + developed a complication

questions.stem: 24h after Burkitt lymphoma chemo → drowsy + altered consciousness (TLS)

choices.label: I
choices.text: ICU+fluid+rasburicase 1. Increased haematocrit + JAK2 mutation 2. 6yo child with frequent painful hand crises 3. 24h after Burkitt lymphoma chemo → drowsy + altered consciousness 4. CLL, 3 days after chemo → dyspnoea + productive cough + fever 5. Patient started ATRA treatment + developed a complication
choices.explanation: TUMOUR LYSIS SYNDROME (TLS): Occurs: 12-72h after chemotherapy for HIGHLY PROLIFERATIVE tumours (Burkitt lymphoma, ALL, high-grade NHL). Metabolic derangements: ↑ uric acid, ↑ K+, ↑ phosphate, ↓ Ca²+ → renal failure, arrhythmia, seizures, death. TREATMENT: (1) ICU monitoring (2) AGGRESSIVE IV FLUIDS (3L/m²/day) → force excretion (3) RASBURICASE (recombinant urate oxidase) → rapidly lowers uric acid (4) Treat hyperkalaemia (calcium gluconate, salbutamol, insulin+glucose) (5) Haemodialysis if renal failure Prevention: allopurinol + IV fluids BEFORE high-risk chemotherapy.

questions.stem: CLL, 3 days after chemo → dyspnoea + productive cough + fever (Febrile Neutropenia)

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: FEBRILE NEUTROPENIA = oncological emergency. Definition: temperature >38°C + neutrophils <0.5 x10■/L (or expected to fall to this). IMMEDIATE broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour: (1) MEROPENEM: broad Gram-negative cover including Pseudomonas (2) VANCOMYCIN: Gram-positive cover (MRSA, streptococcal) Blood cultures x2 (peripheral + line) BEFORE antibiotics. Respiratory symptoms → ALSO consider: PCP (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole), Aspergillus (voriconazole if chest CT shows halo sign).

choices.label: I
choices.text: ICU+fluid+rasburicase 1. Increased haematocrit + JAK2 mutation 2. 6yo child with frequent painful hand crises 3. 24h after Burkitt lymphoma chemo → drowsy + altered consciousness 4. CLL, 3 days after chemo → dyspnoea + productive cough + fever 5. Patient started ATRA treatment + developed a complication

questions.stem: Patient started ATRA treatment → developed a complication (ATRA/Differentiation Syndrome)

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ATRA (All-Trans Retinoic Acid) = treatment for APL (Acute Promyelocytic Leukaemia). DIFFERENTIATION SYNDROME (ATRA syndrome): Occurs within days-weeks of starting ATRA. Features: fever, weight gain, respiratory distress, pleural/pericardial effusion, renal failure, hypotension. Mechanism: rapid differentiation of leukaemic promyelocytes → cytokine release. TREATMENT: DEXAMETHASONE 10mg IV BD immediately. (Prednisolone = equivalent corticosteroid treatment) Continue ATRA in mild-moderate cases; hold in severe cases.

choices.label: I
choices.text: ICU+fluid+rasburicase 1. Increased haematocrit + JAK2 mutation 2. 6yo child with frequent painful hand crises 3. 24h after Burkitt lymphoma chemo → drowsy + altered consciousness 4. CLL, 3 days after chemo → dyspnoea + productive cough + fever 5. Patient started ATRA treatment + developed a complication

questions.stem: "You are the WORST doctor, unlike my PREVIOUS doctor who understood me."

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SPLITTING: inability to integrate positive and negative qualities of self/others → view people as ALL GOOD or ALL BAD. "Worst doctor" (devaluation) vs "previous doctor who understood me" (idealisation) = classic splitting in BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER. The person oscillates between idealisation and devaluation of the same person depending on perceived slights.

choices.label: G
choices.text: humor (+ others) 1. "You are the worst doctor, unlike my previous doctor who understood me." 2. Borderline patient did not attend 7 treatment sessions; doctor stopped treatment. 3. Patient diagnosed with cancer, believes it is just a fever. 4. Man told by wife she is having an affair; he tells her he loves her. 5. Someone slipped and fell; said "In future I will become a skilled figure skier."

questions.stem: Borderline patient skipped 7 sessions; doctor STOPPED TREATMENT.

choices.label: G
choices.text: humor (+ others) 1. "You are the worst doctor, unlike my previous doctor who understood me." 2. Borderline patient did not attend 7 treatment sessions; doctor stopped treatment. 3. Patient diagnosed with cancer, believes it is just a fever. 4. Man told by wife she is having an affair; he tells her he loves her. 5. Someone slipped and fell; said "In future I will become a skilled figure skier."
choices.explanation: ACTING OUT: expressing unconscious conflicts through ACTION instead of reflection. Borderline patient "acting out" by repeatedly missing sessions = unconscious avoidance/testing of abandonment fear. Doctor stopping treatment = potential countertransference reaction (frustration → withdrawal = mirrors patient's fear of abandonment).

questions.stem: Patient diagnosed with CANCER but BELIEVES IT IS JUST A FEVER.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: DENIAL: the most primitive defence. Refusing to acknowledge an anxiety-provoking reality. "Just a fever" = denial of cancer diagnosis. Patient psychologically cannot accept the threat to life. MATURE: acceptance, humour. IMMATURE: denial, splitting, acting out.

choices.label: G
choices.text: humor (+ others) 1. "You are the worst doctor, unlike my previous doctor who understood me." 2. Borderline patient did not attend 7 treatment sessions; doctor stopped treatment. 3. Patient diagnosed with cancer, believes it is just a fever. 4. Man told by wife she is having an affair; he tells her he loves her. 5. Someone slipped and fell; said "In future I will become a skilled figure skier."

questions.stem: Man told by WIFE she is having an AFFAIR. He tells her HE LOVES HER.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: REACTION FORMATION: transforming an unacceptable feeling/impulse into its OPPOSITE. Unacceptable feeling = RAGE/BETRAYAL. Expressed as = "I love you" (opposite emotion). The person genuinely does not experience the anger consciously — it is converted into the opposite affect.

choices.label: G
choices.text: humor (+ others) 1. "You are the worst doctor, unlike my previous doctor who understood me." 2. Borderline patient did not attend 7 treatment sessions; doctor stopped treatment. 3. Patient diagnosed with cancer, believes it is just a fever. 4. Man told by wife she is having an affair; he tells her he loves her. 5. Someone slipped and fell; said "In future I will become a skilled figure skier."

questions.stem: Person SLIPPED AND FELL in front of everyone, said "In the FUTURE I will become a SKILLED FIGURE SKIER."

choices.label: G
choices.text: humor (+ others) 1. "You are the worst doctor, unlike my previous doctor who understood me." 2. Borderline patient did not attend 7 treatment sessions; doctor stopped treatment. 3. Patient diagnosed with cancer, believes it is just a fever. 4. Man told by wife she is having an affair; he tells her he loves her. 5. Someone slipped and fell; said "In future I will become a skilled figure skier."
choices.explanation: HUMOR: using comedy to acknowledge and deal with an anxiety-provoking situation without being overwhelmed by it. The person acknowledges the fall (reality-testing intact) but reframes it humorously as future expertise. MATURE defence mechanism: allows expression of feeling without causing discomfort to others. Mature defences: humour, altruism, sublimation, suppression.

questions.stem: Elevated fetal haemoglobin (HbF)

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: THALASSAEMIA MAJOR: Beta-thalassaemia major: absent/reduced beta globin chains → excess alpha chains → ineffective erythropoiesis. HbF (alpha■ gamma■) = compensatory INCREASE (remains elevated, does not switch to HbA due to absent beta chains). Also elevated HbF: sickle cell disease (especially on hydroxyurea), HPFH (hereditary persistence of foetal haemoglobin). Primary diagnosis here = thalassaemia major.

choices.label: I
choices.text: G6PD deficiency 1. ELEVATED FETAL HAEMOGLOBIN (HbF) 2. Patient with GASTRECTOMY + NON-COMPLIANT with treatment 3. t(9;22) PHILADELPHIA CHROMOSOME 4. ELEVATED aPTT + FACTOR VIII was 1% 5. Child with PAIN IN LEG AND ARM + JAUNDICE + ANAEMIA 6. After ALLOGENEIC BONE MARROW TRANSPLANTATION

questions.stem: Patient with gastrectomy + non-compliant with treatment

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: GASTRECTOMY → removal of PARIETAL CELLS → NO INTRINSIC FACTOR → B12 CANNOT BE ABSORBED in ileum → B12 DEFICIENCY → MEGALOBLASTIC ANAEMIA. NON-COMPLIANT: not taking B12 injections (monthly IM cyanocobalamin required lifelong). Features: macrocytic anaemia, hypersegmented neutrophils, subacute combined degeneration of cord (B12 deficiency), glossitis, neurological symptoms.

choices.label: I
choices.text: G6PD deficiency 1. ELEVATED FETAL HAEMOGLOBIN (HbF) 2. Patient with GASTRECTOMY + NON-COMPLIANT with treatment 3. t(9;22) PHILADELPHIA CHROMOSOME 4. ELEVATED aPTT + FACTOR VIII was 1% 5. Child with PAIN IN LEG AND ARM + JAUNDICE + ANAEMIA 6. After ALLOGENEIC BONE MARROW TRANSPLANTATION

questions.stem: t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: t(9;22) = PHILADELPHIA CHROMOSOME = BCR-ABL1 FUSION GENE. BCR-ABL = constitutively active tyrosine kinase → uncontrolled myeloid proliferation. CML: elevated WBC (often >100), splenomegaly, basophilia, thrombocytosis. Phases: chronic → accelerated → blast crisis. TREATMENT: IMATINIB (tyrosine kinase inhibitor) = targeted therapy revolution. PEARL: Ph chromosome also found in some ALL (Ph+ ALL) — poorer prognosis.

choices.label: I
choices.text: G6PD deficiency 1. ELEVATED FETAL HAEMOGLOBIN (HbF) 2. Patient with GASTRECTOMY + NON-COMPLIANT with treatment 3. t(9;22) PHILADELPHIA CHROMOSOME 4. ELEVATED aPTT + FACTOR VIII was 1% 5. Child with PAIN IN LEG AND ARM + JAUNDICE + ANAEMIA 6. After ALLOGENEIC BONE MARROW TRANSPLANTATION

questions.stem: Elevated aPTT + Factor VIII was 1%

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: HAEMOPHILIA A = X-LINKED RECESSIVE Factor VIII deficiency. FACTOR VIII 1% = SEVERE haemophilia A (normal: 50-150%). Coagulation profile: aPTT PROLONGED (intrinsic pathway affected) | PT NORMAL | Platelets NORMAL | Bleeding time NORMAL. Bleeding: DEEP haemarthroses (knee, elbow), muscle bleeds, intracranial. Treatment: Factor VIII concentrate (recombinant); prophylaxis for severe cases. DESMOPRESSIN for mild haemophilia A (releases stored factor VIII from endothelium).

choices.label: I
choices.text: G6PD deficiency 1. ELEVATED FETAL HAEMOGLOBIN (HbF) 2. Patient with GASTRECTOMY + NON-COMPLIANT with treatment 3. t(9;22) PHILADELPHIA CHROMOSOME 4. ELEVATED aPTT + FACTOR VIII was 1% 5. Child with PAIN IN LEG AND ARM + JAUNDICE + ANAEMIA 6. After ALLOGENEIC BONE MARROW TRANSPLANTATION

questions.stem: Child with pain in leg and arm + jaundice + anaemia

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: SICKLE CELL DISEASE: HbSS (homozygous): valine substituted for glutamic acid at position 6 of beta globin. VASO-OCCLUSIVE CRISIS: sickling → micro-occlusions → BONE PAIN (legs, arms). HAEMOLYTIC ANAEMIA: RBC lifespan reduced (10-20 days) → jaundice (unconjugated bilirubin). CHILD = appropriate age (presents in early childhood after HbF declines at 6 months). Management: analgesics, IV fluids, O2, blood transfusion if Hb critically low.

choices.label: I
choices.text: G6PD deficiency 1. ELEVATED FETAL HAEMOGLOBIN (HbF) 2. Patient with GASTRECTOMY + NON-COMPLIANT with treatment 3. t(9;22) PHILADELPHIA CHROMOSOME 4. ELEVATED aPTT + FACTOR VIII was 1% 5. Child with PAIN IN LEG AND ARM + JAUNDICE + ANAEMIA 6. After ALLOGENEIC BONE MARROW TRANSPLANTATION

questions.stem: After allogeneic bone marrow transplantation

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: GvHD = COMPLICATION OF ALLOGENEIC (donor → recipient) BMT. Mechanism: donor T cells recognise recipient tissues as foreign → attack. ACUTE GvHD (<100 days): skin (maculopapular rash), liver (jaundice), GI (diarrhoea). CHRONIC GvHD (>100 days): multi-system, resembles autoimmune disease (scleroderma-like skin, dry eyes/mouth, bronchiolitis obliterans). Treatment: immunosuppression (steroids, tacrolimus, mycophenolate). GvL (Graft vs Leukaemia) effect = beneficial component of GvHD.

choices.label: I
choices.text: G6PD deficiency 1. ELEVATED FETAL HAEMOGLOBIN (HbF) 2. Patient with GASTRECTOMY + NON-COMPLIANT with treatment 3. t(9;22) PHILADELPHIA CHROMOSOME 4. ELEVATED aPTT + FACTOR VIII was 1% 5. Child with PAIN IN LEG AND ARM + JAUNDICE + ANAEMIA 6. After ALLOGENEIC BONE MARROW TRANSPLANTATION
